sections.title: Your Maven Mental Model, Translated
sections.content:
pom.xml
declares deps
↓
pyproject.toml
mvn install
fetches jars
↓
pip install
~/.m2 repo
global cache
↓
.venv/ (per project)

sections.title: Dependencies Are Per-Project, Not Global — Use a venv
sections.content: Maven isolates dependencies per project automatically. Python's pip installs globally by default, so two projects fighting over requests==2.x vs 2.y corrupt each other. The fix is a virtual environment — an isolated per-project Python with its own packages. Always create one before pip install.

sections.java: # Maven lifecycle mvn clean install mvn dependency:tree # Deps declared in pom.xml, # cached in ~/.m2, scoped to project # by the build tool automatically.
sections.python: # Create + activate an isolated env python -m venv .venv # macOS/Linux: source .venv/bin/activate # Windows PowerShell: .venv\Scripts\Activate.ps1 pip install requests pip freeze > requirements.txt

sections.java: // Importing by fully-qualified package import com.example.service.UserService; import java.util.List; import static java.lang.Math.max; UserService svc = new UserService();
sections.python: # Several import styles import json # whole module from pathlib import Path # one name from math import sqrt as root # alias import numpy as np # common alias

sections.title: Modern Tooling: uv and pyproject.toml
sections.content: The ecosystem is consolidating on pyproject.toml (the pom.xml analog) for declaring dependencies and project metadata. Tools like uv and poetry manage the venv, lockfile, and installs in one command — much closer to the Maven/Gradle experience. uv in particular is dramatically faster than plain pip and worth adopting for new projects.

sections.title: import Runs the Module's Top-Level Code
sections.content: Unlike Java where import is a compile-time reference, a Python import executes the module top to bottom the first time it's loaded. Any code at module level (not inside a function/class) runs as a side effect. This is exactly why the if __name__ == "__main__" guard from Module 01 matters — it keeps script code from firing on import.

sections.java: // Always fully-qualified; package = folder package com.example.app; import com.example.services.UserService; import com.example.models.User; // No relative imports — every path is absolute
sections.python: # Absolute (preferred) — from the package root from myapp.services import UserService from myapp.models import User # Relative — dots mean current / parent package from . import models # same package from ..utils import retry # parent package

sections.title: Circular Imports Bite Harder Than in Java
sections.content: Because import executes a module top-to-bottom, two modules that import each other at the top level can catch one half-initialized — raising ImportError or exposing None attributes. Java resolves circular references lazily and rarely complains. Fixes: import inside the function that needs it, or extract the shared piece into a third module. Tip: use a package's __init__.py to re-export (from .services import UserService) so callers write a clean from myapp import UserService instead of deep paths.

sections.question: Why should you create a virtual environment (venv) before pip installing packages?
options: pip refuses to run without one
pip installs globally by default, so projects can clash over conflicting versions; a venv isolates each project
It's required to use type hints
sections.explanation: Maven scopes dependencies per project automatically, but pip installs into a shared global location by default. Two projects needing different versions of the same library will conflict. A venv gives each project its own isolated interpreter and package set — the per-project isolation Maven gave you for free.
